text: Seneca's evening examination was methodical. In his Letters to Lucilius, he describes interrogating his day under three headings: what bad habit did I cure today, what vice did I resist, and in what respect am I better? The order matters. He begins with cure, not achievement — the Stoic hierarchy places the removal of error above the accumulation of virtue. This structure anticipates what psychologists now call 'process-focused reflection,' which research by Timothy Wilson and colleagues links to more durable behavioral change than outcome-focused review. Seneca's nightly audit was diagnostic, not merely confessional.
question: According to the passage, what does Seneca name as the first of his three evening examination questions?

options.text: What virtue did I practice today?
What bad habit did I cure today?
In what respect am I better?
